talk_title: Risk-based breast cancer screening: evidence, models, and implementation challenges

speaker.name: Graham Colditz
speaker.affiliation: Washington University School of Medicine, St. Louis

MOA_summary: Colditz presented a framework for risk-based breast cancer screening that stratifies women by absolute five‑year risk using calibrated models (e.g., Gail, Tyrer‑Cuzick) and assigns screening frequency accordingly, aiming to increase mortality reduction while decreasing false‑positive workups.
key_findings: Risk‑based screening could improve breast cancer deaths averted while reducing false‑positive workups by up to 25% (CISNET analysis).
No consensus exists on a universal five‑year risk cut‑point for initiating annual screening; proposed cut points: >3% for annual + supplemental imaging, 1.67–3% for annual, <1.67% for biennial.
Implementation challenges include lack of consistent risk thresholds across trials, guideline variability, and need for model‑agnostic frameworks.
open_questions: How can a universally accepted absolute risk threshold be defined for clinical implementation?
What strategies will ensure equitable adoption of risk‑based screening across diverse populations?

cited_papers: JNCI systematic review on image‑based risk models

talk_title: Development and validation of blood‑based biomarkers to guide mammography interval

biomarkers.name: REG4 protein
biomarkers.finding: validated in CHS cohort (p<0.05) with specificity ~95% and sensitivity ~20‑35%

biomarkers.name: JAK2 protein
biomarkers.finding: validated in CHS cohort (p<0.05) with similar performance

models.name: Women's Health Initiative (WHI)
models.genotype_or_key_features: women 50‑69, HR‑positive breast cancer cases, preclinical plasma

models.name: Cardiovascular Health Study (CHS)
models.genotype_or_key_features: women >65, HR‑positive breast cancer cases, preclinical plasma

MOA_summary: Lee described a PROBE‑compliant pipeline that used pre‑clinical plasma from WHI and CHS cohorts to discover protein and auto‑antibody markers (e.g., REG4, JAK2) associated with future hormone‑receptor‑positive breast cancer, then combined them into a four‑marker panel achieving 50% sensitivity at 95% specificity to guide individualized mammography recall intervals.
key_findings: From 3,000 screened proteins and 3,000 auto‑antibodies, 6 protein and 5 auto‑antibody markers met pre‑specified statistical criteria.
A four‑marker panel (including REG4 and JAK2) achieved 50% sensitivity at 95% specificity, exceeding the minimal performance needed to inform six‑month recall.
Validated markers in an independent CHS cohort (p<0.05 for 6 proteins, 5 auto‑antibodies).
Developed clinical‑grade MSD assays for top markers and plan prospective validation in the MD Anderson MERIT cohort (n≈250 cancers).

combination_strategies.rationale: Integrate imaging biomarkers to improve risk prediction beyond blood markers alone
combination_strategies.phenotype: potentially higher AUC and better interval risk stratification

open_questions: Will the four‑marker blood panel retain performance in diverse racial/ethnic populations?
What is the optimal decision rule for combining blood markers with imaging and clinical data?

cited_papers: Pepe et al. 2008 J Natl Cancer Inst (PROBE design)

talk_title: Precision screening and surveillance strategies for colorectal cancer

speaker.name: Mingyang Song
speaker.affiliation: Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health

models.name: Nurses' Health Study / Nurses' Health Study II / Health Professionals Follow‑Up Study
models.genotype_or_key_features: prospective cohort, detailed lifestyle, diet, genetics, CRC outcomes

clinical_data.metric: age to reach benchmark 10‑year CRC risk
clinical_data.value: 44 years for highest‑risk, 56 years for lowest‑risk
clinical_data.population: high‑risk vs low‑risk individuals in NHS/HPFS cohorts

clinical_data.value: persistent risk reduction up to 20 years (RR <1)
clinical_data.population: participants with negative colonoscopy vs no colonoscopy

clinical_data.value: 64% received surveillance; 21% adhered to recommended interval
clinical_data.population: MGB colonoscopy cohort with high‑risk polyps

clinical_data.metric: polygenic risk score association with post‑colonoscopy CRC
clinical_data.population: colonoscoped individuals in NHS/HPFS and UK Biobank

clinical_data.metric: adenoma detection rate (ADR) impact on post‑colonoscopy CRC
clinical_data.value: 3‑fold higher 10‑year CRC incidence for lowest ADR quartile vs highest

MOA_summary: Song presented epidemiologic and genetic risk models that estimate individual 10‑year colorectal cancer risk, showing that high‑risk individuals could start colonoscopy up to six years earlier and low‑risk individuals could delay up to six years; post‑colonoscopy risk varies by polyps, colonoscopy quality (ADR), and polygenic risk scores, supporting personalized surveillance intervals.
key_findings: High‑risk individuals (top risk score) reach the 10‑year CRC risk benchmark at age 44, suggesting screening 6 years earlier than standard age 50.
Low‑risk individuals (bottom risk score) reach the benchmark at age 56, indicating possible delay of screening by 6 years.
Negative colonoscopy confers sustained risk reduction for up to 20 years, but risk varies widely by underlying risk score, allowing interval extension up to 25 years for very low‑risk patients.
Only 64% of patients with high‑risk polyps receive recommended surveillance, and only 21% adhere to the guideline‑specified interval.
Polygenic risk scores add modest predictive value (AUC increase ~0.02) for post‑colonoscopy CRC and polyp recurrence.
Physician adenoma detection rate strongly predicts post‑colonoscopy CRC risk, with a three‑fold difference between lowest and highest ADR quartiles.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Current risk tools have modest AUC (~0.63), limiting cost‑effectiveness of risk‑based screening.
Implementation barriers include algorithm complexity, provider/patient acceptance, and potential to exacerbate disparities.

combination_strategies.rationale: Improve ADR and detect missed lesions, reducing interval cancers
combination_strategies.phenotype: higher ADR associated with lower post‑colonoscopy CRC incidence

combination_strategies.rationale: Capture life‑course exposures not reflected in questionnaire data
combination_strategies.phenotype: potentially improve risk prediction beyond PRS and epidemiologic factors

open_questions: How can risk models achieve higher discrimination (AUC >0.75) to be cost‑effective?
What is the optimal way to integrate PRS, lifestyle, and colonoscopy quality into a single actionable risk score?
How to ensure equitable access to precision colonoscopy recommendations across socioeconomic groups?

cited_papers: Lansdorp‑Vogelaar et al. cost‑effectiveness of risk‑based CRC screening 2023
CISNET breast cancer risk‑based screening analysis 2024
